includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom
John Authers / Bloomberg Points of Return

meta.totals: 18 items from 5 sources, 6 of 6 in-window emails read in full.

items.title: Mira Murati's Inkling: a 975B-param model that only wakes 41B params per query
items.idea: Thinking Machines released Inkling, a 975-billion-parameter open-weights MoE model with only 41 billion parameters active per query — explicitly designed to balance cost against performance rather than chase state-of-the-art benchmarks. It was trained entirely on Nvidia hardware under a multiyear partnership in which Nvidia invested in the startup and committed at least one gigawatt of chips.
items.angle: Nvidia is now backing Nemotron, Reflection AI, AND Thinking Machines simultaneously — that's not picking a winner, it's hedging compute demand across every horse in the open-weights race.
items.tweetA: Nvidia doesn't care who wins the model war — it's now bankrolling Nemotron, Reflection AI, AND Thinking Machines' Inkling. That's not a customer relationship, that's a portfolio hedge on compute demand itself. $NVDA
items.tweetB: Only 41B of Inkling's 975B parameters activate per query. That's the real AI cost story right now — not bigger models, cheaper inference per token. Frontier labs race up, everyone else races down.

charts.desc: No charts in email; text-only Substack post

items.title: Bridgewater fine-tuned a Chinese open model and beat GPT-5 and Claude Opus — at 1/13th the compute cost
items.idea: Bridgewater Associates used Thinking Machines' Tinker platform to fine-tune the Chinese open-weights model Qwen3-235B on its own data. The result outperformed GPT-5 and Claude Opus on financial-document triage while cutting compute costs by more than 13x.
items.angle: The strongest data point yet for 'good enough + fine-tuned + cheap' beating frontier-model APIs on narrow enterprise tasks — bearish for the margin assumptions baked into frontier-lab revenue multiples.
items.tweetA: A hedge fund fine-tuned a CHINESE open model and beat GPT-5 and Claude Opus on document triage, at 13x lower compute cost. If Bridgewater can do this with Tinker, why is anyone paying frontier API prices for narrow enterprise tasks?
items.tweetB: The bear case for frontier-lab enterprise margins isn't 'commoditization is coming someday' — it already happened, quietly, inside one hedge fund's document pipeline. Qwen3 plus fine-tuning beat both frontier labs on their own turf.

charts.desc: No charts in email; text-only Substack post

items.title: Murati compares closed frontier AI to Soviet central planning — and she watched the real thing collapse
items.idea: Thinking Machines' manifesto argues dominant closed-source AI resembles 'central planning' — fine for bounded tasks like chess and math, wrong for the messy real work humans do daily. Murati grew up in Albania and witnessed the collapse of communism as a child, and the company is using that lived experience as the intellectual frame for decentralized, open-weights AI.
items.angle: Ideological framing shapes enterprise procurement — watch whether 'sovereignty over your own model' becomes the dominant enterprise AI sales pitch of 2026, echoing warnings from Karp and Nadella about feeding proprietary data into models you don't control.
items.tweetA: A woman who watched communism collapse as a kid in Albania is now telling enterprises that centralized AI labs are the new central planners. That's not a metaphor she's using loosely — it's the whole pitch for open weights.
items.tweetB: Karp and Nadella have both warned companies against feeding proprietary data into models they don't control. Murati's manifesto just gave that fear a name: 'central planning.' The enterprise AI pitch of 2026 might be sovereignty, not capability.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Deutsche Bank: orbital AI data centers go from 6x the cost of terrestrial today to parity by the early 2030s
items.idea: Deutsche Bank models orbital compute at roughly six times the cost of terrestrial data centers today (excluding compute hardware), narrowing toward 1.0–1.5x by decade's end as Starship reusability and compute density improve, with potential cost parity or even an orbital advantage in the early-to-middle 2030s. The thesis requires the entire SpaceX stack — optical comms, solar generation, active thermal management, dense compute, and cheap reusable launch — to work together at once.
items.angle: If this holds even directionally, it reframes the 'power is the AI buildout bottleneck' narrative — orbit has unlimited solar and no permitting fights, which is the actual scarce resource on Earth right now.
items.tweetA: Everyone's fighting over grid interconnects and gas turbines for AI data centers. Deutsche Bank says by the early 2030s, space might just be cheaper — unlimited solar, no permitting queue, no NIMBY. The power bottleneck might have an escape hatch nobody's pricing in.
items.tweetB: 6x the cost today, roughly 1x by 2030, per Deutsche Bank's SpaceX model. That's the same cost-curve compression AI compute itself went through. The launch industry's Moore's-Law moment might land at exactly the time the AI power crunch needs it most.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Deutsche Bank cost-convergence model likely charted in the full post)

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Index looks calm, single stocks are in crisis: implied vol is near a 15-year high beneath a sleepy tape
items.idea: Goldman's Vickie Chang argues markets are shifting from a 'cleaner' oil/Fed macro regime to a messier one dominated by AI-led growth optimism. Winners and losers within semis, software, memory, and hyperscalers cancel out at the index level, but single-stock implied volatility sits near the top of its 15-year range. A sustained oil-price rise remains the one variable that could drag markets back into a broad, correlated shock.
items.angle: This is a dispersion-trade thesis in one sentence — the VIX looks asleep while the internals are on fire, exactly the setup where dispersion strategies and single-name options outperform index vol.
items.tweetA: The VIX is lying to you right now. Single-stock implied vol is near a 15-year high while the index sits there pretending nothing's happening. Winners and losers in the AI trade are just canceling out at the headline level. Dispersion trader's dream, index-vol seller's trap.
items.tweetB: Goldman's framing: macro used to be the story (oil, Fed), now AI dispersion IS the market. Translation — stop watching SPX vol and start watching semis-vs-software correlation. That's where the actual risk lives.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (implied-vol percentile chart likely in full post)

items.source: John Authers / Bloomberg Points of Return
items.title: Prediction markets now say it's a coin flip whether the Strait of Hormuz reopens by year-end
items.idea: Kalshi's contract on normal Hormuz shipping traffic resuming by end of 2026 has fallen to just under 50/50, dropping sharply this month. Prediction markets have consistently priced the Middle East situation more bearishly than financial markets have — a gap Authers flags as informative despite prediction markets' framing as a covert extension of sports betting.
items.angle: If prediction markets are the leading indicator here and financial markets (which had priced Brent at $70–$80 as recently as the BofA survey window) are lagging, there's an unpriced repricing risk sitting in oil-sensitive assets.
items.tweetA: Kalshi just went to a coin flip on whether Hormuz reopens by year end. Financial markets are still pricing Brent like normal traffic resumes. One of these is wrong, and prediction markets have been the more accurate read on this war for months. $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: Nobody's talking about this: prediction markets have been more bearish than options markets on Hormuz this entire war. If that pattern holds, oil vol is underpriced right now, not overpriced.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — Kalshi Hormuz-reopening-odds chart falling sharply this month, embedded in the original newsletter

items.source: John Authers / Bloomberg Points of Return
items.title: Headline PPI looks great. Strip out energy and the trend is clearly upward, not down.
items.idea: June producer prices showed a sharp headline improvement, but that owed almost entirely to falling energy costs. Ex-energy, every other PPI component trended upward — the opposite of what happened in consumer prices. With energy now rising again as Hormuz stays disrupted, the question of whether businesses eventually pass higher costs to consumers is back on the table.
items.angle: The 'inflation is beaten' narrative currently driving the dollar/rates repricing is riding on an energy base effect that's already reversing in real time — a core-inflation trap reminiscent of 2021's 'transitory' call.
items.tweetA: Everyone's celebrating the PPI headline. Strip out energy and every single component was trending UP, not down — the opposite of the CPI picture. And energy is rising again as we speak. This 'disinflation' story has an expiration date already stamped on it.
items.tweetB: The market repriced the entire rate path off a print where the good news was 100% energy and the underlying trend was bad. That's not disinflation, that's an oil price about to reverse the whole narrative.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (headline vs. core-ex-energy PPI chart)

items.source: John Authers / Bloomberg Points of Return
items.title: 80%+ of global fund managers call semis the most crowded trade ever recorded
items.idea: BofA's July 2–9 fund manager survey — taken as the Middle East situation deteriorated — showed managers at their most bullish on global growth since the post-pandemic surge, cash allocations below 4% (a level BofA treats as a contrarian sell signal), and more than 80% calling semiconductors the world's most crowded trade, the strongest 'crowded' consensus ever recorded in the survey's history.
items.angle: A textbook positioning extreme: max bullishness, minimum cash, maximum agreement on which trade is overcrowded — three signals stacking the same direction right as an exogenous oil shock lands.
items.tweetA: 80% of fund managers agree semis are the most crowded trade ever — the strongest 'we all agree this is crowded' reading BofA has ever recorded. Cash is below 4%, the historical sell signal. That's not a contrarian take, that's consensus admitting it's overextended and doing it anyway.
items.tweetB: Fund managers got maximally bullish on 'no landing' AND maximally long crowded semis in the same week Hormuz shut down. Positioning surveys are supposed to be coincident indicators. This one looks like a warning label nobody read.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — BofA cash-allocation vs. sell-signal threshold, and the % calling semis 'most crowded trade' chart

items.source: John Authers / Bloomberg Points of Return
items.title: The dollar's Iran-driven safe-haven bid is fading as bond markets stop believing the Fed's hawkish tilt
items.idea: The dollar rallied this year on safe-haven flows from the Iran war, but the narrowing gap between US Treasury yields and rest-of-world yields — historically tightly linked to FX performance — signals the dollar is already losing momentum. BofA survey respondents see little further dollar upside even with live geopolitical risk, and a strong majority call the dollar overvalued; Q3 seasonality is also historically negative for the greenback.
items.angle: A safe-haven dollar bid unsupported by a yield differential is fragile — if Q2 earnings strength, not the dollar, ends up carrying US assets, the classic 'US exceptionalism = strong dollar' trade could decouple.
items.tweetA: The dollar's safe-haven bounce from the Iran war isn't backed by the bond market anymore. US-vs-world yield spreads are already narrowing. A currency held up by fear with no rate support underneath is a currency waiting for the fear to fade.
items.tweetB: Strong majority of fund managers: dollar is overvalued. Also strong majority: no more dollar upside even with an active war. When positioning agrees the safe haven is expensive and the catalyst is fading, that's consensus catching up to reality, not a contrarian call anymore.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (yield-spread vs. dollar chart)

items.title: China out-produces US shipbuilding by more than 200x and controls 90%+ of critical minerals processing
items.idea: New Vista Capital's John Lott and Brando Brandolini d'Adda: China controls over 90% of critical minerals processing globally, supplies bearings, batteries, and electronics to both sides of the Ukraine conflict, and out-produces US shipbuilding capacity by more than 200 times. Their framing: the industrial base itself — not any single weapon system — is America's most important strategic and economic asset right now.
items.angle: This is the quantifiable core of the re-industrialization/defense-tech thesis — a 200x shipbuilding gap should reframe how anyone thinks about naval procurement timelines and Taiwan contingency planning.
items.tweetA: China out-produces US shipbuilding by more than 200x. Not 2x, not 20x — 200x. Every conversation about a Taiwan contingency should start from that number, not from carrier-group counts.
items.tweetB: China sells the parts for both sides of the Ukraine war — bearings, batteries, electronics — while controlling 90%+ of critical minerals processing globally. That's not a supply-chain risk anymore, that's a chokehold with a receipt on every drone hitting either trench line.

items.title: It costs 2–3 interceptors to stop one cheap Shahed drone — that math doesn't survive a long war
items.idea: Ukraine and Iran proved kinetic munitions are exhaustible: using two to three expensive interceptors to down one cheap Shahed-style drone is described as 'economic suicide' at scale. The near-term trade, per New Vista, is rebuilding magazine depth while directed energy and electronic warfare gradually take over the defensive layer.
items.angle: Cost-per-kill asymmetry, not interceptor count, is the actual binding constraint on modern air defense — the thesis underlying the entire directed-energy and counter-drone investment wave.
items.tweetA: The real lesson from Ukraine and Iran isn't about drones, it's about arithmetic: burning 2–3 interceptors on a cheap Shahed is a war you lose by bankruptcy before you lose it by attrition. That's the whole investment case for directed energy in one sentence.
items.tweetB: Magazine depth is the new oil reserve. Every dollar spent replenishing interceptor stockpiles today is a bet that directed energy and EW aren't ready yet — and every year that bet gets more expensive to keep making.

items.title: Only a few dozen welders in the US can work on nuclear submarines
items.idea: New Vista portfolio company Deterrence is applying physics-based AI to lift General Dynamics' 155mm artillery shell throughput toward Chinese and Russian production rates — because, per the panel, the US wins factories back through automation, not labor, since the skilled-trade base has collapsed to the point that only a few dozen welders nationally are certified to work on nuclear submarines.
items.angle: This explains why the entire defense-industrial reshoring thesis has to be an automation story, not a jobs story — there's no labor pool to reshore into even if the capital shows up.
items.tweetA: A few dozen welders. That's the entire pool of Americans certified to work on nuclear submarines. You can appropriate all the defense budget you want — there's no labor supply curve to solve this with. It has to be automation or it doesn't happen.
items.tweetB: Everyone talks about reshoring as a jobs story. The actual constraint is there's no one left to hire — the skilled trades that build submarines and shells have shrunk to a headcount you could fit in a school bus. That's why 'AI for manufacturing' funding is suddenly a defense thesis.

items.title: A single nuclear detonation in low earth orbit could erase GPS and commercial connectivity
items.idea: New Vista frames space as an active theater rather than a science experiment: Starship-scale launch cadence, orbital data centers, and contested satellite activity mean a single nuclear detonation in low earth orbit (an EMP-style event) could wipe out GPS and commercial satellite connectivity globally. They call space infrastructure one of the largest unpriced opportunities of the next decade.
items.angle: Pairs directly with the SpaceX orbital-data-center thesis above — both point to space becoming genuine critical infrastructure, which means it also becomes a genuine target.
items.tweetA: One nuclear detonation in low orbit and GPS goes dark worldwide. We're simultaneously planning to put AI data centers up there. Space isn't a moonshot narrative anymore, it's critical infrastructure — which means it's also a target.
items.tweetB: Everyone's pricing satellites like telecom infrastructure. New Vista's framing: it's the most contested, least defended, most catastrophically fragile layer of the entire economy. One EMP event and half the positioning/comms stack goes dark at once.

items.title: July hike odds collapsed from 40%+ to 10-13% in three sessions — the fastest rate-path reversal of the cycle
items.idea: June PPI fell 0.3% MoM (vs. flat consensus) with core PPI +0.2% (vs. +0.4% expected), confirming the prior day's CPI miss (-0.4% MoM, the biggest single-month drop since April 2020). CME FedWatch July 29 hike odds collapsed to 10-13% from above 40% on Monday. September hike odds, however, are still ~51%, meaning the market killed one meeting, not the hiking cycle.
items.angle: Pairs directly with the Authers PPI item above — the whole disinflation move rides an energy base effect already reversing with Hormuz shut and a fourth day of US strikes on Iran, so July's relief and September's ~51% odds are both live and in tension.
items.tweetA: July hike odds went from 40%+ to 10-13% in three days — fastest repricing of the entire cycle. But September odds are still parked at 51%. The market didn't kill the hiking cycle, it moved the fight to the next meeting, and Hormuz is currently deciding which side wins it.
items.tweetB: Biggest CPI drop since April 2020, and it happened the exact week energy prices were about to reverse. This isn't 'inflation is beaten,' it's 'inflation was captured mid-swing on the way back up.' September's the real test.

charts.desc: Charts inside the source article — worth opening (FedWatch probability chart)

items.title: BTC broke $65k on a $230M short squeeze while Fear & Greed stayed at 25 — that's covering, not conviction
items.idea: Bitcoin broke above $65,000 for the first time in weeks, driven by roughly $230M in forced short liquidations across two sessions ($376M total across 85,000+ traders). But the Fear & Greed Index sits at just 25 (still 'Extreme Fear') despite the 3.2% market gain — the classic signature of a mechanical squeeze rather than organic accumulation. BTC's correlation with equities hit a record 0.96 in the same window.
items.angle: When price rises and fear stays pinned to the floor, forced buyers are covering, not new buyers accumulating — a fragile rally that needs sustained ETF inflows (ETH drew +$58M) to become real instead of mechanical.
items.tweetA: BTC broke $65k on $230M of forced short covering while the Fear & Greed Index stayed stuck at 25 — Extreme Fear, during a rally. That's the clearest 'don't trust this breakout' signal crypto has, and it's flashing right now.
items.tweetB: BTC's correlation to stocks just hit a record 0.96. Crypto isn't a hedge against equity risk anymore, it's a leveraged replica of it — 92% of its price action is now just the Nasdaq with extra steps.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — Fear & Greed vs. price divergence, liquidation heatmap referenced in source

items.title: Japan's Upper House just advanced a bill reclassifying Bitcoin as a financial instrument — spot ETFs possible by 2027
items.idea: Japan's Upper House committee approved legislation to reclassify Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies as financial instruments, clearing a path toward spot crypto ETFs as early as 2027. Described as the strongest pro-crypto regulatory signal from a major economy this year, though it still faces additional legislative steps. Meanwhile India stays cautious and the EU pushes ahead with its Digital Euro pilot instead.
items.angle: The world's third-largest economy moving toward spot crypto ETF approval is a multi-year demand-channel story, not a today trade — worth tracking alongside the regulatory divergence across Japan, India, and the EU.
items.tweetA: World's 3rd-largest economy just took a real step toward spot Bitcoin ETFs. Nobody's pricing in what happens to flows when Japan's massive retail savings pool gets a regulated on-ramp — this is a 2027 story that starts mattering well before 2027.
items.tweetB: Regulatory divergence is the underrated crypto story right now: Japan advancing spot ETFs, India staying cautious, the EU building a Digital Euro instead of embracing stablecoins. Three of the world's biggest economies, three completely different bets on who controls settlement rails.

items.title: Stripe + Advent's $53B bid for PayPal is a stablecoin land-grab wearing a payments-M&A costume
items.idea: Stripe and Advent International offered $60.50/share for PayPal (a 28% premium, PYPL +21% premarket to $57.30), valuing the company above $53B with roughly $50B in committed bank financing and Stripe/Advent holding equal stakes. The explicit driver: combining two payments giants' stablecoin and blockchain-settlement ambitions — PayPal's PYUSD stablecoin already carries a $2.8B market cap.
items.angle: The biggest tell yet that stablecoin settlement rails, not card-network economics, are now the strategic prize in payments M&A — expect Visa/Mastercard and bank-owned rails to respond.
items.tweetA: $53 billion, $50 billion of it borrowed, to buy a company mostly so you can control its stablecoin. PYUSD has a $2.8B market cap — a rounding error next to the deal size. Stripe isn't buying PayPal's payments business, it's buying a settlement rail nobody else has built at scale yet.
items.tweetB: The largest payments deal of the year just told you where the real value is moving: not transaction fees, stablecoin settlement. If Visa and Mastercard don't answer this within 12 months, they've conceded the next decade of payments infrastructure.

items.title: The AI momentum trade is having its worst month since December 2023 — and crypto is now 96% correlated to it
items.idea: The Dow Jones US Thematic Market Neutral Momentum Index (tracking AI favorites) is headed for its worst month since December 2023, even as headline indices stay calm — echoing the Goldman dispersion thesis above. With Bitcoin's stock correlation at a record 0.96, a cracking AI momentum tape is now directly a crypto risk factor, not just an equity one.
items.angle: A cross-asset contagion channel worth watching explicitly: AI momentum drawdown feeds crypto drawdown via the correlation regime, independent of anything crypto-specific.
items.tweetA: If you're long crypto because you think it's diversifying away from tech risk, look at the correlation number again: 0.96. The AI momentum index just had its worst month since December 2023. Congratulations, you're long AI momentum with extra steps.
items.tweetB: Two data points from two different sources, same week: single-stock vol near 15-year highs, AI momentum index worst month since Dec 2023. Read together, that's the AI trade actually cracking underneath a calm-looking tape.
